title: Why 43% of Physiotherapy Care Is Non-Recommended — and What Clinics Can Do About It
meta_description: Research reveals significant gaps in physiotherapy care delivery. Learn how objective measurement and standardised protocols can reduce clinical variation.
html_content:
The Scale of the Problem: When Evidence Meets Practice

The figure is stark: up to 43% of physiotherapy care delivered in clinical practice does not align with current evidence-based recommendations. This statistic represents more than academic concern — it highlights a fundamental disconnect between what research tells us works and what happens in everyday clinical practice.

The gap manifests in multiple ways: inconsistent rehabilitation timelines, variable exercise selection, and subjective assessment methods that introduce significant clinician bias. While individual physiotherapists may deliver excellent care, the absence of standardised measurement creates unnecessary variation in treatment quality and outcomes.

This variation has measurable consequences. Patients receiving evidence-aligned care demonstrate superior outcomes, faster recovery times, and lower re-injury rates. The challenge lies not in knowledge — most clinicians understand current best practice — but in consistent implementation across different practitioners, conditions, and settings.

Protocol Adherence: The Foundation of Consistent Care

Evidence-based rehabilitation protocols provide the framework for consistent care delivery, yet their implementation remains highly variable. Research consistently demonstrates superior outcomes when structured protocols guide treatment decisions, but adherence rates vary significantly between clinicians and conditions.

Post-surgical rehabilitation exemplifies this challenge. Following ACL reconstruction, structured protocols incorporating specific exercise progressions and objective milestones produce more predictable outcomes (Fontanier et al., 2025). However, without standardised measurement tools, clinicians often rely on subjective assessments that introduce variation in progression decisions.

Similarly, rotator cuff repair rehabilitation benefits from structured approaches that balance tissue protection with progressive loading (Bandara et al., 2021). The evidence supports specific timelines and exercise progressions, yet implementation varies widely based on individual clinician preferences rather than objective patient responses.

Protocol adherence improves when clinicians have access to objective measurement data. Clear benchmarks for strength, range of motion, and functional capacity remove guesswork from progression decisions and ensure patients advance through rehabilitation phases based on physiological readiness rather than arbitrary timelines.

The Measurement Gap: Why Subjective Assessment Fails Consistency

Over 90% of physiotherapists rely primarily on subjective strength testing, yet this approach introduces significant measurement error and reduces treatment consistency. Manual muscle testing, while clinically expedient, cannot detect strength deficits below 30-40% and provides no quantitative data for progression tracking.

This measurement gap creates cascading problems throughout the care pathway. Without objective baselines, clinicians cannot accurately gauge treatment effectiveness or make data-driven adjustments. Patients progress through rehabilitation based on time-based protocols rather than physiological readiness, leading to suboptimal outcomes.

The solution requires moving beyond traditional assessment methods towards standardised, objective measurement protocols. Simple tools — not expensive equipment — can provide the quantitative data needed for consistent clinical decision-making. A basic dynamometer costs less than £200 but transforms strength assessment from guesswork into precise measurement.

Objective measurement also improves patient engagement. When patients see quantitative progress — 15% strength improvement over three weeks, range of motion increased by 20 degrees — they understand their recovery trajectory. This transparency builds confidence and compliance while providing clear targets for continued improvement.

Technology Integration: Evidence From Recent Trials

Recent research demonstrates how technology can bridge the evidence-practice gap without disrupting clinical workflow. Digital physiotherapy platforms show comparable outcomes to traditional care while improving consistency and reducing variation (Pak et al., 2023).

The key lies not in replacing clinical expertise but in providing infrastructure that supports evidence-based decision-making. Technology platforms that incorporate objective measurement protocols, standardised exercise progressions, and outcome tracking enable clinicians to deliver consistent care regardless of experience level or caseload pressures.

Blood flow restriction training exemplifies how specific interventions can be systematically implemented across different conditions and settings (Hughes et al., 2017). When protocols are standardised and measurement is objective, clinicians can confidently integrate advanced techniques into routine practice.

However, technology adoption must be evidence-driven rather than novelty-focused. The most effective solutions integrate seamlessly into existing workflows while providing clear clinical value. Complex systems that require significant behaviour change often fail to achieve sustained adoption.

Systematic Implementation: Building Consistency Into Practice

Reducing non-recommended care requires systematic changes to practice structure, not just individual clinician education. Successful implementation focuses on three core elements: standardised assessment protocols, objective measurement tools, and structured outcome tracking.

Assessment standardisation begins with condition-specific testing batteries that provide quantitative data for clinical decision-making. For shoulder conditions, this might include objective strength testing for external rotation, internal rotation, and abduction (Hsieh et al., 2023). For knee conditions, quantitative assessment of quadriceps strength, hop performance, and range of motion provides the foundation for evidence-based progression.

Implementation should follow a phased approach:

  • Phase 1: Introduce objective measurement for primary outcome variables (strength, range of motion)
  • Phase 2: Implement standardised exercise progressions based on objective criteria
  • Phase 3: Establish outcome benchmarks and variation monitoring
  • Phase 4: Integrate patient-reported outcomes with objective measures

This systematic approach ensures changes are sustainable and clinically meaningful. Attempting to transform entire practice patterns simultaneously often fails; incremental implementation with clear clinical benefits builds momentum for broader adoption.

The Clinical and Economic Case for Change

Reducing non-recommended care delivers benefits across multiple dimensions: improved patient outcomes, reduced treatment variation, and enhanced practice efficiency. When care aligns with evidence, patients recover faster and experience fewer complications, reducing overall treatment burden.

From a practice perspective, consistent care delivery simplifies staffing, training, and quality assurance. When all clinicians follow evidence-based protocols with objective measurement, treatment quality becomes predictable rather than variable. This consistency enables practices to confidently contract for outcomes rather than just activity.

The economic benefits extend beyond immediate cost savings. Practices that demonstrate consistent, evidence-based care can justify premium pricing and attract value-based contracts. Insurance providers increasingly recognise the cost-effectiveness of standardised, measurement-driven rehabilitation.

Most importantly, consistent care builds patient trust and clinical confidence. When outcomes are predictable and transparent, both patients and clinicians have clear expectations for recovery timelines and functional improvements.

Next Steps: Moving From Awareness to Action

Understanding the scale of non-recommended care is the first step; systematic implementation of measurement-driven protocols is the solution. Start with one condition or patient population, introduce objective measurement tools, and establish clear progression criteria based on quantitative data rather than subjective assessment.

The goal is not perfect care delivery overnight but consistent improvement in treatment quality and outcomes. Every objective measurement, standardised protocol, and evidence-based decision reduces the 43% figure and improves patient care. Focus on building sustainable systems that support clinical excellence rather than depending on individual clinician variability.

References

  1. Fontanier V, Vergonjeanne M, Eon P et al. Effect of open kinetic chain exercises during the first weeks of anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction rehabilitation: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Physical therapy in sport : official journal of the Association of Chartered Physiotherapists in Sports Medicine. 2025;72:95-108. PubMed
  2. Bandara U, An VVG, Imani S et al. Rehabilitation protocols following rotator cuff repair: a meta-analysis of current evidence. ANZ journal of surgery. 2021;91(12):2773-2779. PubMed
  3. Pak SS, Janela D, Freitas N et al. Comparing Digital to Conventional Physical Therapy for Chronic Shoulder Pain: Randomized Controlled Trial. Journal of medical Internet research. 2023;25:e49236. PubMed
  4. Hughes L, Paton B, Rosenblatt B et al. Blood flow restriction training in clinical musculoskeletal rehabilitation: a systematic review and meta-analysis. British journal of sports medicine. 2017;51(13):1003-1011. PubMed
  5. Hsieh LF, Kuo YC, Huang YH et al. Comparison of corticosteroid injection, physiotherapy and combined treatment for patients with chronic subacromial bursitis - A randomised controlled trial. Clinical rehabilitation. 2023;37(9):1189-1200. PubMed

faq.q: What causes such high rates of non-recommended physiotherapy care?
faq.a: The main factors include reliance on subjective assessment methods, inconsistent protocol implementation, and lack of standardised measurement tools that introduce significant variation between clinicians.

faq.q: How can clinics implement objective measurement without expensive equipment?
faq.a: Simple tools like basic dynamometers, goniometers, and standardised functional tests provide quantitative data for under £500, transforming assessment from subjective guesswork to precise measurement.

faq.q: What are the business benefits of reducing non-recommended care?
faq.a: Consistent, evidence-based care enables predictable outcomes, justifies premium pricing, attracts value-based contracts, and reduces treatment variation that improves practice efficiency and patient satisfaction.

original_topic: Why 43% of Physiotherapy Care Is Non-Recommended — and What Clinics Can Do About It
